smartlead_campaign: OR #3 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
hey Leilani, noticed LPC Golf's founder never touched leather or a sewing machine before starting, curious how that came about.

on a separate note: I built 3 meta ad mockups from LPC's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook from your site and reviews.

 no pitch attached, it's already built.

 should I send it?

- Kyle

 P.S. you gotta show people what an amazing brand you got for real!!

thread.subject: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Leilani - the part most brands react to isn't even the ads, it's the brand playbook. it lays out your voice, the exact language your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's all set to send alongside the mockups. want me to send both over?

- Kyle

I am open to a conversation.  

 Best, 
Leilani

Love it - as promised in my first email, here are your 3 ad mockups built from LPC's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Hi Kyle,

I've booked some time on our calendar for Monday.

I had one quick piece of feedback on the mockups: the golf bag in image 2 appears way too small in comparison to the rest of the objects, but the others look great.

Also, just as a heads-up, our site is a custom Hydrogen build. I'm a software engineer, so I can implement changes myself, but please note that it is not a standard Shopify template.

Best,
Leilani

Leilani - fair call on the bag scale. the mockups were built from public site assets only, so think of them as angle concepts - with your actual product files they get a lot sharper. good problem to have.

 Hydrogen build is no issue at all, and having a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds everything up.

 talk Monday :)

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: LPC is a premium handmade leather golf headcover brand out of San Francisco that has never run Meta ads, so this call is about starting clean from their three mockups, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A small artisan brand making handcrafted leather golf headcovers. Legal name is Little Prince Customs. Hand-stitched by one maker named Karl. Founded around 2022. Site is a custom Hydrogen build, not a normal Shopify template, and Leilani the contact is a software engineer who can make site changes herself.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Premium custom leather golf headcovers, hand-stitched, made in San Francisco. Three collections are named on the site: Quilted (20 designs, hand-sewn diamond patterns), Exotic Classic (2 designs, embossed cowhide), and Artisan (8 designs, signature classics plus rare-material pieces). They also offer custom embroidery like initials, logos, and designs. No prices are public, so do not quote any.
deep_dive.positioning: Top, heritage-craft end of the golf accessory market. Handmade and personal, not mass-produced. The whole pitch is craftsmanship you can be proud of on the course, built to last and made for you.
deep_dive.who_buys: Golfers who care about style and quality and will pay more for something handmade that stands out. Gift buyers are likely too, since embroidery and initials suit gifting, but that is a guess to confirm on the call.
customer_voice: These headcovers are absolutely amazing! The craftsmanship is top-notch and I get compliments on them every time I'm on the course.
strengths: Clear, strong story: one artisan named Karl, hand-stitched, made in the USA. That is easy to build scroll-stopping ads around.
Real product variety across three named collections, plus custom embroidery, which gives us plenty of angles and a natural way to sell sets.
gaps: Almost no social proof on the site. Only one visible customer quote. Ads need trust, and right now there is very little to point to.
No prices, no catalog, and no ads history we can see. We are starting from zero and cannot judge margin or order value until Leilani tells us.

game_plan.headline: Start from the three Scroll-Stopper ads, prove the Quilted collection can sell cold on Meta, then widen and add custom embroidery as the second play.

phases.goal: Prove that one ad can bring in a sale from cold traffic at a price that makes sense. This month is about learning, not scale.
campaigns: One simple sales campaign aimed at cold golfers who like premium gear.
A small retargeting campaign for people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Lead with the Quilted collection. Reason: it has 20 designs, far more than the other two collections, which usually means it is the range they make and sell most. More designs also means we can test which look grabs attention without changing the product story.
phases.creative: Go live first with the strongest of the three Scroll-Stopper ads that leans on the Karl and hand-stitched story, since that is the clearest reason to pay a premium. Do not launch the mockup with the small golf bag (image 2) until we rebuild it with real product files, per Leilani's own note. Rework the other two into clean versions once we have her product photos.
phases.budget: Start small and steady so we can read results without burning cash.
phases.what_success_looks_like: At least one ad shows cheap clicks and steady add-to-carts, and we get one or more sales we can trace to the ads. If nothing sells, we learn whether the block is the offer, the price, or the lack of reviews.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the ad and audience that worked in month one, and widen who sees it.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold campaign with more budget.
Test new audiences: lookalikes from month one buyers and site visitors, plus interest groups around golf and gifting.
Keep retargeting running and add customer reviews collected in month one.
lead_products: Keep Quilted as the workhorse. Introduce the Artisan collection (8 designs) as a higher-end pull for people who want something rarer.
phases.creative: Refresh the winning ad so it does not go stale. Add any new customer quotes or photos we gather. Push the 'build a set, save 10% with 4 or more' offer here, since it lifts order size once people trust the brand.
phases.budget: Step budget up gradually, only on what earned it in month one.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad keeps bringing sales at a similar or better return as we spend more, and at least one new audience works.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and add a second product angle for repeat and higher-value orders.
campaigns: Scale the proven cold campaign further.
Launch a dedicated custom embroidery campaign (initials, logos, designs) as the second play.
Add a gifting angle if the holiday timing or discovery answers support it.
lead_products: Custom embroidery as the second product. Reason: it is the most personal thing they offer, hard to copy, and matches their 'made for you' promise. It also suits gift buyers and can justify a higher price.
phases.creative: Build fresh ads around personalization and the 'as unique as you' message. Show initials and logos being stitched. Keep the Karl craft story as the trust anchor.
phases.budget: Scale further only where the return holds. Hold back from the mockup we never fixed unless rebuilt.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products are now selling from ads, order size is up from the set discount and embroidery, and we have a repeatable setup we can keep feeding.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: About $100 to $150 a day to start
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 25 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs for a premium physical product, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience work without guessing.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: Cannot give an honest figure until we know their price and order size
estimates.basis: No prices or catalog are public, so any revenue number would be made up. Once Leilani shares average order value and margin, we can set a real target off the starting budget and a sensible return goal.

assumptions: Quilted is their bestseller because it has by far the most designs. If Leilani says another collection sells most, we lead with that instead.
Their price and margin are high enough to carry paid ads. If the order value is low, the whole plan and budget change.
They can fulfill more orders. Karl hand-stitches everything, so if he is one person, scaling ads could outrun what he can make. We need to know their capacity.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta or Instagram ads before, even a boosted post?
questions.listening_for: We think the answer is no ads are running now. Confirm it. If they tried before and stopped, ask what happened, since that tells us what already did not work.

questions.question: Who handles marketing today, and how much time do you have for this?
questions.listening_for: Is Leilani the decision maker or is Karl. Since she is a software engineer, she may want to be hands-on. Good for site changes, but confirm they will not slow approvals.

questions.question: What does a headcover sell for, and roughly what does it cost you to make one?
questions.listening_for: The single most important answer. Price and margin decide if ads can work at all and set the real budget. Do not guess this. Get the number.

questions.question: How many headcovers can Karl make in a week?
questions.listening_for: If it is one person hand-stitching, there is a ceiling. We must not sell more than he can build. This shapes how hard we scale in months two and three.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site, what percentage roughly ends up buying, and where do people drop off?
questions.listening_for: If lots of people visit but few buy, the block may be price, trust, or the site, not the ads. Since it is a custom Hydrogen build, checkout quirks are possible.

questions.question: Which collection or design sells the most right now, Quilted, Artisan, or Exotic Classic?
questions.listening_for: We assumed Quilted because it has the most designs. If they say otherwise, we change the lead product for month one.

questions.question: Do you have customer reviews or photos anywhere, even in DMs or email, that are not on the site?
questions.listening_for: The site shows almost no reviews. Ads need trust. If they have happy customers off-site, we can use that fast. If they truly have none, that changes month one.

questions.question: How much of your business is people buying for themselves versus buying as gifts?
questions.listening_for: This decides how heavy we go on the embroidery and gifting angle in month three, and whether we time anything to holidays.

questions.question: Do you ever get customers who found you by searching things like 'custom golf headcovers' or 'leather headcovers'?
questions.listening_for: People do search for golf headcovers, so there is a real opening for Google Search ads and SEO later. If they say buyers already find them by searching, that is a strong signal to raise Google in a future phase.

questions.question: Are you showing up when people search your own brand name or 'LPC Golf'?
questions.listening_for: If competitors bid on their name or they rank poorly, a small branded Google campaign is cheap protection. Only worth raising once Meta is proving out.

objections.objection: We have never run ads and are not sure they work for something this premium and handmade.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small and prove one ad first before spending real money. Premium handmade is actually easier to advertise, because the Karl story and the craft give us something most golf brands do not have. Month one is a low-cost test, not a big bet.

objections.objection: Karl makes everything by hand, so we cannot handle a flood of orders.
objections.answer: Good, that means we control the tap. We set spend to match what Karl can build, and we can lean on the higher-priced custom pieces so each order is worth more without needing huge volume. We scale only as fast as you can make.

objections.objection: One of your mockups had the golf bag sized wrong, so can I trust the creative?
objections.answer: Fair, and you caught it yourself. Those were built from public site images only, so treat them as angle concepts. With your real product files they get sharper, and we will not run that image until it is rebuilt right. The other two you already said look great.

objections.objection: Our site is a custom Hydrogen build, not standard Shopify, will your tracking and setup even work?
objections.answer: No problem at all, and having you as a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds it up. We tell you exactly what to place and where, and you can implement it cleanly yourself instead of fighting a template.

red_flags.flag: Almost no social proof. Only one customer quote is visible and no reviews on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold ads live or die on trust. A premium price with no visible proof makes strangers hesitate. We need reviews or photos before or during month one, or the ads will struggle.

red_flags.flag: No prices or catalog are public.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot judge if the order value and margin can carry paid ads. Until Leilani gives real numbers, any budget or revenue target is a guess, and we must not state one.

red_flags.flag: Everything is hand-stitched by one person, Karl.
red_flags.why_it_matters: There is a hard limit on how many orders can be filled. If ads work too well, they could sell more than he can make and end up with angry customers and refunds.

red_flags.flag: Custom items cannot be returned, and the customer pays return shipping.
red_flags.why_it_matters: This can lower conversion and raise complaints if not made clear in the ad and on the product page. We should message it plainly so buyers know before they order.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads later. People do search for terms like 'custom golf headcovers' and 'leather golf headcovers', so there is real demand to capture once Meta is proving out. Confirm with the discovery question before promising it.
A small branded Google campaign to protect the LPC Golf name in search, if they are not already showing up first for it.
